2.title: About us
2.author: Pavla Civínová
2.excerpt: We promote the interests of the Pilsen Region in the European Commission, the European Parliament and the Committee of the Regions. We monitor the development of EU policies that have a significant impact on our region. We raise awareness of our region in Brussels by connecting cultures and businesses. History of the Representation The European […]
2.content:
We promote the interests of the Pilsen Region in the European Commission, the European Parliament and the Committee of the Regions. We monitor the development of EU policies that have a significant impact on our region. We raise awareness of our region in Brussels by connecting cultures and businesses.

History of the Representation The European Office of the Pilsen Region in Brussels began its activities on 3 January 2006 as a detached workplace of the Regional Authority of the Pilsen Region. Its first representative was Karel Lizerot. To facilitate the start of its operations, colleagues from the East Midlands European Office (East Midlands also includes the Northamptonshire region, which is a partner region of the Plzeň Region), based at Avenue d’Auderghem 22–28, 1040 Brussels, provided office facilities. The office was based there until June 2007, when it moved to its own rented premises in the so-called Czech House at Rue du Trône 60, B-1050 Brussels. The Czech House in Brussels was a project related to the first-ever Czech Presidency of the Council of the EU, which took place in the first half of 2009. For this purpose, the Permanent Representation of the Czech Republic to the EU needed to increase the number of diplomats and secure appropriate premises. These were found in a building directly adjacent to the Permanent Representation at Rue Caroly 15, where the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Czech Republic, as the founder of the Permanent Representation, rented the entire office building. However, the Permanent Representation required only part of the building, so the remaining premises were offered to other Czech offices in Brussels. As a result, the offices of Czech Airlines, Czech Railways, CzechTrade/CzechInvest, CzechTourism, the Czech Centre, the Czech Business Representation to the EU (CEBRE), the Embassy of the Czech Republic in Belgium, and a “regional floor” for Czech regions moved under one roof. Not all regions made use of this opportunity. The following remained outside:
  • The Capital City of Prague, as it has had its own premises in Brussels at Avenue Palmerston 16 since June 2002;
  • The Central Bohemian Region, which at that time rented its own building at Avenue d’Auderghem 45, Brussels-Etterbeek;
  • The South Bohemian Region and the Liberec Region, which sublet offices from the Central Bohemian Region;
  • The Zlin and Olomouc Regions, which were represented by a lobbying agency.
The Czech House therefore hosted the offices of the South Moravian Region, Karlovy Vary Region, Hradec Kralove Region, Moravian-Silesian Region, Pardubice Region, Pilsen Region, Usti Region and the Vysocina Region. At that time, the representative of the Pilsen Region in Brussels was Mgr. Zbyněk Prokop, who took over the agenda from Karel Lizerot on 1 January 2007.

The Czech House project in Brussels, which aimed to provide shared premises for all Czech offices in Brussels under favourable conditions, was a very good idea—unfortunately too good to survive. The Czech Republic did not complete the purchase of the rented building, and the Czech House project in Brussels ended in 2011. Where to Find Us and How to Reach Us The Office of the Pilsen Region is located at Place de Jamblinne de Meux 31, Brussels – Schaerbeek, B-1030. The building is close to the headquarters of the European Commission and the Council of the EU at Rond-Point Schuman, and not far from the European Parliament at Rue Wiertz and the Committee of the Regions at Rue Belliard. From the airport, the easiest way is by express bus no. 12 (Meiser or Schuman stop) or no. 21 (stop almost in front of the building – Jamblinne de Meux). By metro, the nearest station is Schuman on lines 1 and 5, followed by approximately a 10-minute walk or a short bus ride (bus no. 21) for two stops to Jamblinne de Meux. Najdete nás na adrese Place de Jamblinne de Meux 31 v Bruselu

Contact: Permanent Representation of the Pilsen Region in Brussels – Mgr. Zbyněk Prokop Place de Jamblinne de Meux 31, B-1030 Brussels – Schaerbeek, Belgium Tel.: +420 377 195 583 Mobile: +420 724 951 679 or +32 473 530 951 E-mail: info@zpkb.eu Office hours: Monday–Friday 9:00–12:00, 13:00–17:00. If you wish to visit the Office of the Pilsen Region in Brussels, we recommend arranging an appointment in advance. Due to participation in conferences, working meetings, or travel to the PilsenRegion (usually one week per month), it may happen that no one is present during an unannounced visit (there is no permanent reception service).

40.title: Contacts
40.author: Pavla Civínová
40.excerpt:
Where can you find us?
What are our office hours?
40.content:
Contacts

The Permanent Representation of the Pilsen Region in Brussels is headed by Mgr. Zbyněk Prokop.
 

Would you like to visit us?

Office hours: Monday – Friday: 9:00–12:00, 13:00–17:00

If you plan to visit the Pilsen Region Office in Brussels, we recommend arranging an appointment in advance. Due to attendance at various conferences, working meetings, or travel to the Pilsen Region (usually one week per month), it may happen that no one is present in the office during an unannounced visit, as there is no permanent reception service.

 

Where can you find us?

The Office of the Pilsen Region is located at Place de Jamblinne de Meux 31, Brussels – Schaerbeek, B-1030. The building is close to the headquarters of the European Commission and the Council of the EU at Rond-Point Schuman, and not far from the European Parliament on Rue Wiertz and the Committee of the Regions on Rue Belliard.

From the airport, the easiest way is to take express bus No. 12 (stop Meiser or Schuman) or bus No. 21 (the stop Jamblinne de Meux is almost directly in front of the building).

The nearest metro station is Schuman on lines 1 and 5. From there it is about a 10-minute walk, or you can take bus No. 21 for two stops to Jamblinne de Meux.

107.title: Internships for students
107.author: Pavla Civínová
107.excerpt: Students and graduates, are you interested in doing an internship with us? Read on to find out how our internship works and how to apply.
107.content: At the Representation of the Pilsen Region in Brussels, both students and recent university graduates can undertake an internship. We have been offering these internships since 2008, and to date, we have welcomed over sixty interns. The following lines are intended primarily for Czech students; therefore, we do not provide additional information in English here.

189.title: Interests of the Pilsen Region
189.author: Pavla Civínová
189.excerpt: We promote the interests of the Pilsen Region where European decisions are made. At the Representation of the Pilsen Region in Brussels, we promote our region’s interests where European decisions are made. We monitor EU policies, assess their impact on the Pilsen Region, and connect cultures and businesses. Our goal is to ensure the region’s […]
189.content:
We promote the interests of the Pilsen Region where European decisions are made.

At the Representation of the Pilsen Region in Brussels, we promote our region’s interests where European decisions are made. We monitor EU policies, assess their impact on the Pilsen Region, and connect cultures and businesses. Our goal is to ensure the region’s voice is heard in Europe and that EU policies support its development.

200.title: Students in Brussels
200.author: Pavla Civínová
200.excerpt: The aim of the excursion was to give students a closer understanding of how the EU works. Which institutions did the students visit? Who did they meet at the Pilsen House?
200.content: From March 3 to March 5, 2025, another trip to Brussels took place, this time by the Student Parliament of the Pilsen Region, organized by the Radovánek Leisure Center. The aim of the excursion was to give students a closer understanding of how European institutions function. They therefore visited the Parlamentarium as well as the headquarters of the European Committee of the Regions. The group also came to see the office of the Representation of the Pilsen Region in Brussels, where, in the representative premises, two Czech Members of the European Parliament were “grilled” by the students for two hours: Tomáš Zdechovský (European People’s Party / KDU-ČSL) and Ondřej Dostál (non-attached / Stačilo!). They were joined by former regional governor and member of the Committee of the Regions Rudolf Špoták (Greens and Progressives / independent) and Deputy Governor Martin Rybár (KDU-ČSL). The questions students asked the attending politicians clearly showed that they are interested in public affairs and that developments in our country are not indifferent to them. The following day, before departing for home, the students also visited the historic center of Brussels and the iconic model of an iron atom enlarged 165 billion times – the Atomium.

208.title: The Future of European Funds
208.author: Pavla Civínová
208.excerpt: Cohesion. In other words, cohesion policy and how European funds will be distributed. This was the topic of the event, which was also attended by Minister Petr Kulhánek.
208.content: On 27 March 2025, the House of the Representation of the Pilsen Region in Brussels hosted an important event, the significance of which was underlined by the presence of the Czech Minister for Regional Development, Petr Kulhánek. The event presented the Czech Republic’s position on the future of cohesion policy for the period 2028–2035, in other words, the stance with which the country is entering the European debate on how EU funds will be distributed in the future. Within the European Union, the Czech Republic belongs to the group of so-called “net recipient” countries, meaning those that receive more from the European budget than they contribute. For ordinary citizens, this is also evident from how often they see signs at larger construction sites stating that the ongoing work is co-financed by the European Union.   Within this group of “net recipient” countries, the Czech Republic is also considered one of the “good performers.” This means that the country has been achieving economic growth, to which European financial support has undoubtedly contributed. The future of this support is therefore absolutely crucial for us—not only for the country as a whole, but also for us as the Pilsen Region and for our municipalities. For this reason, the discussion with the minister was so important, and we believe it was beneficial for both sides. The views and recommendations presented from our side are based on many years of practical experience with drawing on European funds. These perspectives were primarily conveyed to the minister and his delegation by the Deputy Governor of the Pilsen Region for European Affairs and IT, Martin Rybár.

215.title: Back to the past
215.author: Pavla Civínová
215.excerpt: We have been active in Brussels since 2006. Over this long period, we have welcomed many politicians, businesspeople, as well as personalities from the arts. Do you recognize them?
215.content:
Back to the past. During our time in Brussels (since 2006), we’ve welcomed a number of distinguished guests to the Pilsen House — the office of the Representation of the Pilsen Region. From politicians and artists to business leaders from our region. In short: we connect the Pilsen Region with Europe.

Who can you recognize? Some of these photos are already true museum pieces.

374.title: Personal Data Protection Policy
374.author: admin
374.excerpt: Statement on the Processing of Personal Data Statement on the processing of personal data pursuant to the Regulation (EU) 2016/679 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 27 April 2016 on the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and on the free movement of such data, and […]
374.content:
Statement on the Processing of Personal Data

Statement on the processing of personal data pursuant to the Regulation (EU) 2016/679 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 27 April 2016 on the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and on the free movement of such data, and repealing Directive 95/46/EC (hereinafter the “General Regulation”), and information for data subjects.

Data Controller

Pilsen Region, Regional Office of the Pilsen Region, Company ID: 70890366, with registered office at Škroupova 18, 306 13 Pilsen (hereinafter the “Controller”), hereby informs data subjects, in accordance with Article 12 of the General Regulation, about the processing of personal data and their rights.

Scope of Personal Data Processing

Personal data are processed to the extent provided to the Controller by the relevant data subject, in connection with entering into a contractual or other legal relationship with the Controller, or data which the Controller has otherwise collected and processes in accordance with applicable legal regulations or to fulfill the Controller’s statutory obligations.

Sources of Personal Data

  • Directly from the data subject (e.g., contact form on the Pilsenň Region website);
  • From other public or state authorities;
  • Non-public registers (ISEO, ROB);
  • Publicly accessible registers, directories, lists, and records (ROS, RÚIAN, commercial register, trade register, land registry, etc.).

Categories of Personal Data Processed

  • Address and identification data enabling unambiguous identification of the data subject (e.g., name, surname, title, date of birth, permanent address, Company ID, Tax ID) and contact details (e.g., address, phone number, fax, email, or similar information);
  • Descriptive data (e.g., bank account details);
  • Other data necessary to perform a contract;
  • Data provided beyond legal requirements processed on the basis of consent from the data subject (e.g., processing photographs, use of personal data for HR purposes, etc.).

Categories of Data Subjects

  • Employees of the Controller;
  • Supervisory authorities;
  • Other persons in a contractual relationship with the Controller;
  • Job applicants.

Categories of Recipients of Personal Data

  • Supervisory authorities;
  • Tax authorities;
  • Public administration bodies;
  • Processor;
  • State and other authorities in the fulfillment of legal obligations;
  • Other recipients.

Purpose of Personal Data Processing

  • Purposes specified in the data subject’s consent;
  • Negotiation of contractual relationships;
  • Performance of a contract;
  • Protection of rights of the Controller, recipients, or other affected persons (e.g., debt recovery);
  • Archiving based on legal requirements;
  • Recruitment for job vacancies;
  • Compliance with statutory obligations of the Controller;
  • Protection of vital interests of the data subject.
  • Method of Processing and Protection of Personal Data

Personal data are processed by the Controller. Processing is carried out at the Controller’s registered office by authorized employees, or, where applicable, by a processor. Processing occurs via IT systems or manually for paper records, ensuring full compliance with security principles for the management and processing of personal data. The Controller has implemented technical and organizational measures to ensure data protection, particularly to prevent unauthorized or accidental access, alteration, destruction, loss, unlawful transfer, or other misuse of personal data. All parties with access to personal data respect the privacy rights of data subjects and are obliged to comply with applicable legal regulations on data protection.

The Controller commits to processing personal data in accordance with applicable laws and to use the data only to the extent necessary to fulfill the purposes for which the data were collected, always ensuring no harm to the data subject.

Where personal data are to be disclosed to third parties and consent is required, the data subject will be asked for consent, e.g., when concluding a contract.

Retention Period of Personal Data

In accordance with the periods specified in relevant contracts, the Controller’s filing and retention rules, or applicable legal regulations, personal data will be retained only as long as necessary to ensure the rights and obligations arising from contractual relationships or statutory requirements.

Information to Data Subjects

The Controller processes personal data based on the consent of the data subject (which may be given for one or more specific purposes) except in cases where the law allows processing without consent.

In accordance with Article 6(1) of the General Regulation, the Controller may process data without consent where:

  • Processing is necessary to perform a contract with the data subject, or to take steps prior to entering into a contract at the request of the data subject;
  • Processing is necessary to comply with a legal obligation of the Controller;
  • Processing is necessary to protect the vital interests of the data subject or another natural person;
  • Processing is necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the Controller;
  • Processing is necessary for the legitimate interests of the Controller or a third party, except where such interests are overridden by the rights and fundamental freedoms of the data subject requiring protection of personal data.

Rights of Data Subjects

In accordance with Article 12 of the General Regulation, the Controller informs data subjects, upon request, of their right to access personal data and the following information:

  • Purpose of processing;
  • Categories of personal data concerned;
  • Recipients or categories of recipients to whom personal data have been or will be disclosed;
  • Planned retention period;
  • All available information about the source of the personal data if not obtained directly from the data subject;
  • Whether automated decision-making, including profiling, occurs.

Any data subject who finds or believes that the Controller or Processor processes their personal data in a way that violates their privacy or the law, particularly if the data are inaccurate for the purposes of processing, may:

  • Request an explanation from the Controller;
  • Request the Controller to rectify the situation, including correction, supplementation, or deletion of personal data. If the request is found justified, the Controller will immediately correct the issue.

Requests must always be properly assessed. If there is doubt about the identity of the person requesting information, the Controller may request additional information to confirm the data subject’s identity, in accordance with Article 12(6) of the General Regulation.

For requests under Articles 15–22 of the General Regulation, information about measures taken must be provided without undue delay, and in any case within one month of receipt. In exceptional cases, the period may be extended by two months, and the data subject must be informed, including the reasons for the extension.

All information and actions under Articles 13, 14, 15–22, and 34 of the General Regulation are provided free of charge. Only if requests are manifestly unfounded or excessive, particularly repetitive, may a reasonable fee be charged, or the request may be refused, with proper justification.

If the Controller does not comply with the data subject’s request, the data subject has the right to contact the supervisory authority, i.e., the Office for Personal Data Protection, directly. The data subject may do so without first contacting the Controller.

Data Protection Officer

Contact for the Data Protection Officer of the Regional Office of the Pilsen Region:
Mgr. Milan Švarc, Head of Internal Audit Unit, Data Protection Officer
Tel.: +420 377 195 751
E-mail: milan.svarc@plzensky-kraj.cz

376.title: Cookie Settings
376.author: admin
376.content:
This website does not collect or use any cookies or other tracking technologies to store information about visitors. No personal data are collected, stored, or processed through cookies.

All website content is accessible without registration or storing data on your device. Your privacy and the security of your information are our top priorities.

If in the future cookies are implemented on the website, you will be informed through an updated statement and will have the opportunity to give or refuse your consent to their use.
